originalTitle: Jesus Christ Superstar review – Sam Ryder raises the roof in rock opera turned up to 11
summary: The Eurovision star leads a glittery production of Andrew Lloyd Webber and Tim Rice’s blockbuster but this booming show lacks context and clarity
content: This amped-up version of Andrew Lloyd Webber and Tim Rice’s rock opera is presented by the same triumvirate who near enough blew the critics – and crowds – to heaven with their 2016 revival. Tim Sheader is again director, designer Tom Scutt’s set has a similar scaffold structure (but with some audience members standing around it this time) and Drew McOnie is once more the choreographer. The production, in spirit, goes back not just to the first century when Jesus of Nazareth rises to become an inspiring preacher, radical tearaway and thorn in the side of the temple clerics, but also to the 1970s era of hippy-dom and flower power from which the original show arose. Plenty of glitter is hurled around the stage but there is actual stardust in the cast as Sam Ryder plays the messiah (in man-bun and floaty shirt combo). Tyrone Huntley, who earned an Olivier award nomination for his 2016 Judas, reprises the role of a brooder who thinks Jesus has let fame go to his head. The camped-up Herod (Jesse Tyler Ferguson) brings a Drag Race aesthetic, with trailing gold gown for King Herod’s Song. The role will be played by a bevy of celebrities including Boy George, Layton Williams and Julian Clary over the course of the run. It’s a stock crowd-pulling tactic for a West End show – if Ryder were not enough. But the first thing that hits you here is the sound, which contains Greek levels of melodrama. It’s a shame it stays at a single, fever-pitched high, flattening the emotional drama. Still, there is the glory of the signature song, Superstar, and the vocal blast of both Ryder and Huntley is a sound to behold. Rock and electric guitar crisscrosses choral church music and there are flecks of jazz and gospel. The voices really do raise the roof, especially Ryder’s. He gets a standing ovation for Gethsemane, Jesus’s confrontation with God. But the characterisation is far less satisfying and, just like the singer Self Esteem in David Hare’s Teeth ‘n’ Smiles, Ryder’s accomplishment is primarily vocal here. His Jesus does not seem like the dangerous revolutionary that temple High Priest Caiaphas (Bob Harms) regards him to be but rather a nice guy, verging on the bland. Huntley emotes much better alongside the songs and brings an edge but it seems a little one-sided. The friction between these two central players does not take off dramatically. Ryder’s portrayal is so woolly that you don’t know what Jesus stands for. So it becomes difficult to pinpoint what, exactly, Judas dislikes: is it that Jesus has gone too far, as Judas repeats, or not far enough? He suggests that Jesus has lost sight of Judea’s occupation by the Romans, and of the poverty around them. These are eternally unanswered questions around the figure of Judas but it still feels like a flaw in characterisation. The volume makes it all the more unclear as lyrics cannot always be heard. Ryder tries to bring it down but his words then become a too-soft murmur. The stripped back nature of the set design renders some scenes very abstract, too, such as Jesus’s attack on trade at the temple. The ensemble is choreographed into feverish delirium or devotion. They could represent members of the early church or those of American mega-churches today, yet the milieu never quite shakes off its nostalgic sense of hippy-dom. It is partly inspired by the 1973 film, which is full of psychedelia. Mary Magdalene (Desmonda Cathabel) stays reminiscent of a flower child, anodyne and there to soothe Jesus. Her songs are full of soupy romance, such as I Don’t Know How to Love Him; what a missed opportunity for a transgressive biblical character. The story is located in a non-specific time, without enough context to charge it. And the audience standing on the stage never feels integrated or implicated as the crowds that betray, or back, Jesus on his way to the cross. The violence of his end is certainly captured with an imaginative use of glitter but the focus is squarely spectacle and sound. Still, there’s no doubt fans of Ryder will love it. At London Palladium until 5 September
chineseTitle: 《耶稣基督万世巨星》评论——萨姆·莱德在摇滚歌剧中的地位达到了 11
chineseSummary: 这位欧洲电视网明星领衔主演了安德鲁·劳埃德·韦伯和蒂姆·赖斯的大片，但这部蓬勃发展的节目缺乏背景和清晰度
chineseContent: 这部安德鲁·劳埃德·韦伯和蒂姆·赖斯的摇滚歌剧的增强版由同一个三人组演出，他们在 2016 年的重演几乎让评论家和观众震惊不已。蒂姆·谢德再次担任导演，设计师汤姆·斯卡特的布景也有类似的脚手架结构（但这次有一些观众站在它周围），德鲁·麦奥尼再次担任编舞。这部作品在精神上不仅可以追溯到一世纪，当时拿撒勒人耶稣崛起成为一位鼓舞人心的传教士，对圣殿神职人员来说是彻底的撕裂和眼中钉，而且还可以追溯到 1970 年代嬉皮士时代和最初的表演诞生的花朵力量时代。舞台上到处都是闪闪发光的东西，但当萨姆·莱德（Sam Ryder）扮演弥赛亚（穿着男士发髻和飘逸衬衫组合）时，演员阵容中确实有星尘。泰隆·亨特利 (Tyrone Huntley) 凭借 2016 年的《犹大》获得了奥利弗奖提名，他再次扮演了一个沉思者的角色，他认为耶稣让名声冲昏了他的头脑。神气十足的希律王（杰西·泰勒·弗格森饰）带来了变装皇后秀的美感，金色拖尾长袍伴随着希律王之歌。在跑步过程中，这个角色将由包括男孩乔治、雷顿·威廉姆斯和朱利安·克拉里在内的众多名人扮演。如果莱德还不够的话，这是伦敦西区演出的一种惯用的吸引观众的策略。但这里首先打动你的是声音，其中包含希腊情节的情节。遗憾的是它停留在单一的、狂热的高度，使情感戏剧变得平淡。尽管如此，标志性歌曲《Superstar》依然光彩照人，莱德和亨特利的声音也令人惊叹。摇滚乐和电吉他与教堂合唱音乐交错，还有爵士乐和福音音乐的点缀。这些声音确实让人兴奋不已，尤其是莱德的声音。他得到了一个站立的机会
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
